title: On Embeddings for Numerical Features in Tabular Deep Learning
authors: Yury Gorishniy, Ivan Rubachev, Artem Babenko (Yandex; HSE)
tokenization_method: Per-feature scalar->vector embedding modules built from two new primitives: (1) Piecewise Linear Encoding (PLE), a continuous, ordinal generalization of one-hot/thermometer over feature bins; (2) Periodic embeddings, concat[sin(v),cos(v)] with v=2*pi*c*x and learnable frequencies c. Both are optionally followed by Linear and ReLU layers, giving named modules L/LR/Q/T/Q-LR/T-LR/P/PL/PLR. The framework embeds each numerical feature independently with the same functional form but never shares parameters across features.
feature_types_handled: numerical (continuous) only — this is the core contribution. Categorical features use standard learned per-category embeddings (unchanged from FT-Transformer). No temporal handling, no explicit missing-value handling.
token_granularity: per-feature: one embedding vector z_i = f_i(x_i) in R^{d_i} per numerical feature. For Transformer backbones each feature embedding is one token; for MLP/ResNet all feature embeddings are concatenated into one flat vector.
numerical_strategy: Two families. PLE(x) is a length-T vector: component e_t = 0 if x<b_{t-1} (t>1), = 1 if x>=b_t (t<T), else (x-b_{t-1})/(b_t-b_{t-1}); i.e. a 'soft' ordinal one-hot where the active bin holds a linear ramp in [0,1] and all lower bins are 1. Bins from quantiles (PLEq, unsupervised) or from a per-feature decision tree on the target (PLEt, target-aware, C4.5-style). Periodic(x)=concat[sin(2*pi*c x), cos(2*pi*c x)] with c~N(0,sigma), sigma and k tuned. Best single recipe overall is PLR = ReLU . Linear . Periodic.
categorical_strategy: Standard learned embedding table per categorical feature (inherited from FT-Transformer); not a contribution of this paper.
high_cardinality_handling: None specific. High-cardinality categoricals are out of scope of this work; benchmark datasets are mostly numerical (0-8 categorical features).
temporal_handling: none — datasets are static i.i.d. tabular; no time-varying features modeled.
scales_to_many_features: Tested up to 200 numerical features (SA) and 136 (MI). BUT the paper explicitly evaluates 'only the best metric values without taking efficiency into account', so it is NOT a scaling demonstration. Per-feature embeddings multiply parameter count by feature count: Table 7 shows up to ~250x (CA), ~2000x (CH, small data) param blowup for embedding modules; one token per feature means O(F^2) attention cost in the Transformer backbone. Authors note size overhead does not fully translate to runtime overhead, and MLP/ResNet + embeddings stay faster than Transformers.
downstream_model: MLP, ResNet, and Transformer (Transformer-L == FT-Transformer). Key message: embeddings are backbone-agnostic and benefit non-Transformer backbones.
pretraining_objective: none — fully supervised, trained end-to-end with the backbone. PLEt bins are fit once before training using train-split labels (a target-aware preprocessing step, not a pretraining objective). Quantile bins fit on train split only.
key_results: On 11 mid/large GBDT-friendly public datasets (CA, AD, HI, etc.), proper numerical embeddings close most of the DL-vs-GBDT gap. MLP-PLR reaches avg rank 3.0 vs MLP 8.5, beating XGBoost (4.6) and rivaling CatBoost (3.6); first time DL matches GBDT on California-Housing and Adult. Embedded MLP/ResNet perform on par with attention models. PLR gives best average; PLE (Q-LR/T-LR) is a strong, cheaper, interpretable alternative.
baselines_compared: CatBoost, XGBoost (GBDT ensembles); vanilla MLP/ResNet/Transformer; FT-Transformer (Transformer-L); AutoDis (Guo et al. 2021 CTR numerical embedding); thermometer/binary and one-blob binning encodings (ablation).
transferable_idea: Replace the raw-scalar / single-linear numerical input with a richer per-feature embedding — default to PLR (periodic+linear+ReLU); use quantile- or tree-based PLE when interpretability, cheaper compute, or preprocessing-robustness matters. This is the most directly applicable backbone for the numerical half of a 70+-feature tokenizer.
limitations: Same functional form applied to every feature (authors flag as likely suboptimal); no mechanistic explanation of why embeddings help optimization; numerical-only (no categorical/temporal/missing innovation); efficiency deliberately ignored in the headline comparison; sigma (periodic) is a sensitive hyperparameter requiring tuning; tree-based vs quantile-bin superiority is benchmark-biased (GBDT-friendly) and explicitly NOT claimed to generalize.
pitfalls: (1) Per-feature, non-shared embeddings cause large parameter blowup and one-token-per-feature sequence length — at 70+ features this is the dominant cost the paper does not solve. (2) PLEt builds bins from a decision tree on TRAIN labels; if bins are refit on the full set or per-fold leakage is not controlled, target leakage results (matches proposal anti-pattern). (3) The Periodic trick does NOT transfer to GBDT/XGBoost (Table 10) — it is DL-specific, so do not assume embedding gains are model-agnostic feature engineering. (4) A single shared sigma / single bin scheme across heterogeneous features may be suboptimal when feature distributions differ widely (the topic's exact setting).
relevance_to_topic: Defines the canonical numerical-feature tokenizer (PLE / Periodic / PLR) that any 70+-mixed-feature deep tabular tokenizer should use for its continuous half; also surfaces the per-feature parameter-blowup and sequence-length cost that scaling to many features must address.
code_available: yes — https://github.com/yandex-research/tabular-dl-num-embeddings (official, has PLE/Periodic/PLR modules; PLR later folded into the rtdl-num-embeddings library).
